questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Direction of angular velocity and angular acceleration
questions.structural_anchor: Direction of angular velocity and angular acceleration
questions.question: A wheel rotates counterclockwise as viewed from above. Using the right-hand rule, what is the direction of the angular velocity vector?
questions.hint: Curl your right-hand fingers in the direction of rotation; your thumb points in the direction of ω.
questions.explanation: For counterclockwise rotation viewed from above, curling right-hand fingers counterclockwise makes the thumb point upward (out of the page). Option B would be correct for clockwise rotation.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Direction of angular velocity and angular acceleration
source_trace.structural_element: Direction of angular velocity and angular acceleration
source_trace.content_anchor: right-hand rule: fingers in direction of rotation, thumb points in direction of ω

explanations_tiered.definition: Angular velocity direction is given by the right-hand rule: curl fingers in rotation direction, thumb points along ω.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Counterclockwise rotation (viewed from above) produces angular velocity vector pointing upward (out of the page).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If a wheel spins counterclockwise as seen from above, point your right-hand thumb up — that's the direction of ω.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Applying the right-hand rule: for counterclockwise rotation, the ω vector points along the axis toward the observer (upward).
explanations_tiered.advanced: ω = dθ/dt in vector form; direction follows the right-hand rule from the axis of rotation, perpendicular to the plane of rotation.
reasoning_steps: Curl right-hand fingers in that direction (counterclockwise).
That is the direction of ω.
misconceptions: Confusing counterclockwise with clockwise direction for ω.
Thinking ω points in the direction of tangential velocity (it points along axis).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: CCW from above → ω up (out of page).

glossary_entry.definition: Curl fingers in rotation direction, thumb points in direction of ω.

flashcard.front: A wheel rotates counterclockwise as viewed from above. What is the direction of ω?
flashcard.back: Upward (out of the page) by the right-hand rule.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Counterclockwise rotation from above: direction of ω?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Upward (out of page) by the right-hand rule.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Curl fingers in rotation direction, thumb points along ω.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate with a physical spinning object and hand gesture.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Right-hand rule: CCW → ω out of page.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What is the formula for centripetal force required to keep an object of mass \( m \) moving in a circle of radius \( r \) at constant speed \( v \)?
questions.hint: Centripetal force is mass times centripetal acceleration (\( a_c = v^2/r \)).
questions.explanation: Centripetal force \( F_c = m a_c = m v^2 / r \). Option A inverts v² and r, option B uses v instead of v², and option D incorrectly multiplies by r instead of dividing.

explanations_tiered.definition: Centripetal force is the net inward force causing uniform circular motion, given by \( F_c = m v^2 / r \).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Centripetal acceleration \( a_c = v^2/r \); Newton's second law gives \( F_c = m a_c = m v^2 / r \).
explanations_tiered.beginner: To swing a ball in a circle, you must pull it inward. The faster it goes or the tighter the circle, the harder you must pull.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Derived from \( a_c = v^2/r \) (radial acceleration) and \( F = ma \).
reasoning_steps: Apply Newton's second law: \( F = ma \).

formula_analysis.name: Centripetal force
formula_analysis.derivation: From a_c = v²/r and F = ma.

misconceptions: Thinking centripetal force is a separate force (it is the net inward force from existing forces).
Using \( F_c = m v / r \) (missing square on v).

glossary_entry.definition: Net inward force causing uniform circular motion; F_c = mv²/r.

flashcard.front: What is the formula for centripetal force?
flashcard.back: F_c = mv²/r, where m = mass, v = speed, r = radius.

publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe Newton's law of universal gravitation

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Earth's gravity provides the centripetal force that keeps the Moon in orbit. If the Moon's orbital radius increased, what would happen to the required centripetal force (assuming constant orbital speed)?
questions.hint: Centripetal force is inversely proportional to radius when speed is constant: \( F_c \propto 1/r \).
questions.explanation: From \( F_c = m v^2 / r \), if \( v \) is constant and \( r \) increases, \( F_c \) decreases (inverse relationship). Option A would be true if angular velocity were constant (\( F_c = m \omega^2 r \propto r \)), but the question specifies constant speed, not constant angular velocity.

explanations_tiered.definition: Centripetal force for constant speed circular motion is inversely proportional to radius: \( F_c \propto 1/r \).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: With fixed mass and speed, increasing the orbital radius reduces the required centripetal force because the curvature is gentler.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you swing a ball on a longer string at the same speed, you don't have to pull as hard inward as with a short string.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From \( F_c = mv^2/r \), m and v constant, F_c ∝ 1/r. Larger r means smaller F_c.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For a given v, the centripetal acceleration \( v^2/r \) decreases as r increases, so the required net inward force decreases.
reasoning_steps: If r increases, denominator increases, so F_c decreases.

formula_analysis.name: Centripetal force (constant v)
formula_analysis.derivation: From Newton's second law and centripetal acceleration.

misconceptions: Confusing with F_c = m ω² r (constant ω case, where F_c ∝ r).
Assuming larger orbit always means larger force (opposite for constant speed).

glossary_entry.definition: For constant speed, F_c ∝ 1/r; larger radius → smaller centripetal force.

flashcard.front: Moon's orbital radius increases, speed constant. What happens to centripetal force from gravity?
flashcard.back: It decreases. F_c = mv²/r → larger r, smaller F_c.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Moon's orbit radius increases, speed constant. Centripetal force?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare with constant ω case: F_c ∝ r.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply equations to solve problems related to Newton's law of universal gravitation
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. F_c = mv²/r, constant v → r↑ → F_c↓.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A projectile is launched with speed \( v_0 = 30 \, \text{m/s} \) at angle \( \theta = 30^\circ \). What is its maximum height? (\( g = 10 \, \text{m/s}^2 \))
questions.explanation: \( H = (30^2 \times \sin^2 30^\circ) / (2 \times 10) = (900 \times 0.25) / 20 = 225 / 20 = 11.25 \, \text{m} \). Option B forgets the ½ factor in denominator, option C uses sin θ instead of sin²θ.

explanations_tiered.definition: Maximum height is the highest vertical position reached by a projectile above launch point.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Using H = v₀² sin²θ/(2g), substitute given values to find 11.25 m.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The ball goes up 11.25 m at its peak — about as high as a three-story building.
explanations_tiered.advanced: From vertical motion: v_y² = (v₀ sin θ)² - 2gH, set v_y = 0 → H = (v₀ sin θ)²/(2g).

formula_analysis.name: Maximum height
formula_analysis.derivation: From v_y² = v_{0y}² - 2gΔy with v_y = 0 at top.

misconceptions: Forgetting to square sin θ (using sin θ instead of sin²θ).
Using 2g in numerator incorrectly.

glossary_entry.definition: Highest point of projectile trajectory; H = v₀² sin²θ / (2g).

flashcard.front: Find max height: v₀=30 m/s, θ=30°, g=10 m/s².

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Projectile: v₀=30 m/s, θ=30°, g=10 m/s². Find H.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce that sin²θ is (sin θ)², not sin(θ²).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply equations to solve problems related to projectile motion

questions.subject: Physics
questions.structural_anchor: Horizontal range and maximum height of a projectile
questions.question: For the projectile in Q34 (v₀ = 30 m/s, θ = 30°, g = 10 m/s²), what is the horizontal range?
questions.explanation: \( R = (30^2 \times \sin 60°) / 10 = (900 \times 0.866) / 10 = 779.4 / 10 = 77.94 \approx 77.9 \, \text{m} \). Option A uses \( \sin 30° \) instead of \( \sin 60° \), option D uses \( v_0^2/g \) (range at 45°).

source_trace.structural_element: Horizontal range and maximum height of a projectile

explanations_tiered.definition: Horizontal range is the total horizontal distance traveled by a projectile on level ground.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Using R = v₀² sin 2θ / g, with sin 60° = 0.866, gives R ≈ 77.9 m.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The ball lands about 78 meters away — roughly three-quarters of a football field.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Derived from R = v₀ₓ × t_total = (v₀ cos θ) × (2 v₀ sin θ / g) = (v₀² × 2 sin θ cos θ)/g = v₀² sin 2θ / g.

formula_analysis.name: Horizontal range (level ground)
formula_analysis.derivation: R = v₀ₓ × t_total = (v₀ cos θ) × (2v₀ sin θ / g).

misconceptions: Using sin θ instead of sin 2θ in range formula.
Forgetting to convert angle to radians for calculator (sin of degrees works with degree mode).

glossary_entry.definition: Total horizontal distance; R = v₀² sin 2θ / g.

flashcard.front: Find range: v₀=30 m/s, θ=30°, g=10 m/s².

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Find R for projectile in Q34 (v₀=30 m/s, θ=30°, g=10 m/s²).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare with H from Q34: H = 11.25 m, R = 77.9 m.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply equations to solve problems related to projectile motion

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: According to Kepler's first law, the Sun is located at which point in a planet's elliptical orbit?
questions.hint: An ellipse has two special points inside called foci.
questions.explanation: Kepler's first law states that the orbit of each planet is an ellipse with the Sun at one focus. Option A describes a circle (special case of ellipse with both foci coincident), but general ellipses have Sun at a focus, not center.

source_trace.content_anchor: the orbit of a planet around the Sun is an ellipse with the Sun at one focus

explanations_tiered.definition: Kepler's first law (law of ellipses): planets orbit the Sun in ellipses with the Sun at one focus.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The Sun is not at the center of the elliptical orbit but at one of the two foci, causing the planet's distance to vary.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine a stretched circle (ellipse) with the Sun off-center, closer to one end. That's where the Sun sits.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: An ellipse has two focal points. Kepler discovered that the Sun occupies one focus, and the planet orbits such that r₁ + r₂ = constant.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Mathematically, the polar equation of an ellipse with Sun at focus is r = a(1 - e²)/(1 + e cos θ), where e is eccentricity.
reasoning_steps: Identify that the Sun is at a focus.
Not at center, not at endpoints of axes.
misconceptions: Thinking the Sun is at the center of the orbit (circular orbit misconception).
Confusing focus with center or vertex of ellipse.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Kepler I: Sun at one focus of elliptical orbit.

glossary_entry.definition: Planetary orbits are ellipses with the Sun at one focus.

flashcard.front: Where is the Sun located in a planet's elliptical orbit (Kepler I)?
flashcard.back: At one of the two foci of the ellipse.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Where does the Sun lie in a planet's elliptical orbit?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Sun at one focus of ellipse.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Draw an ellipse with foci and show Sun at one.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Sun at one focus of the ellipse.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Equation of motion for constant angular acceleration
questions.structural_anchor: Equation of motion for constant angular acceleration
questions.question: A wheel rotates with constant angular acceleration \( \alpha = 4.0 \, \text{rad/s}^2 \). It starts with \( \omega_0 = 2.0 \, \text{rad/s} \). What is the angular speed after \( t = 3.0 \, \text{s} \)?
questions.explanation: \( \omega_f = 2.0 + (4.0 \times 3.0) = 2.0 + 12.0 = 14.0 \, \text{rad/s} \). Option A omits initial ω₀, option B uses \( \alpha t^2 \) incorrectly.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Equation of motion for constant angular acceleration
source_trace.structural_element: Equation of motion for constant angular acceleration

explanations_tiered.definition: Final angular velocity under constant angular acceleration is ωf = ω₀ + αt.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Add the change in angular velocity (αt) to the initial angular velocity.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If a wheel is already spinning at 2 rad/s and speeds up by 4 rad/s each second, after 3 seconds it adds 12 rad/s to reach 14 rad/s.
explanations_tiered.advanced: From dω/dt = α, integrate ∫ dω = ∫ α dt gives ω = ω₀ + αt.
worked_solution: Step 2: ω₀ = 2.0 rad/s, α = 4.0 rad/s², t = 3.0 s
misconceptions: Forgetting to add initial ω₀ (using just αt).
Using αt² (displacement formula) instead of αt.

flashcard.front: ω₀=2 rad/s, α=4 rad/s², t=3 s. Find ωf.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Wheel: ω₀=2 rad/s, α=4 rad/s², t=3 s. Find ωf.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply equations to solve problems related to rotational motion

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Equation of motion for constant angular acceleration
questions.structural_anchor: Equation of motion for constant angular acceleration
questions.question: Using the same wheel as Q37 (ω₀ = 2.0 rad/s, α = 4.0 rad/s²), what is the angular displacement after 3.0 s?
questions.explanation: \( \Delta \theta = (2.0 \times 3.0) + \frac{1}{2} \times 4.0 \times (3.0)^2 = 6.0 + 0.5 \times 4.0 \times 9 = 6.0 + 18.0 = 24.0 \, \text{rad} \). Option A omits the ω₀t term, option C uses αt² without ½.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Equation of motion for constant angular acceleration
source_trace.structural_element: Equation of motion for constant angular acceleration

explanations_tiered.definition: Angular displacement under constant angular acceleration is Δθ = ω₀t + ½αt².
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: First term (ω₀t) is displacement from initial speed, second term (½αt²) is additional displacement from acceleration.
explanations_tiered.beginner: In 3 seconds, the wheel turns 6 radians from its starting spin, plus another 18 radians from speeding up, for a total of 24 radians.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Integrate ω(t) = ω₀ + αt: θ(t) - θ₀ = ∫₀ᵗ (ω₀ + αt) dt = ω₀t + ½αt².
misconceptions: Forgetting the ω₀t term (displacement from initial velocity).
Omitting the ½ factor in the αt² term.

flashcard.front: Wheel: ω₀=2 rad/s, α=4 rad/s², t=3 s. Find Δθ.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Same wheel: ω₀=2, α=4, t=3. Find Δθ.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare with Q38 and Q37: both terms matter.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply equations to solve problems related to rotational motion

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A torque of \( 20.0 \, \text{N·m} \) is applied to a wheel with moment of inertia \( I = 4.0 \, \text{kg·m}^2 \). What is the angular acceleration?
questions.explanation: \( \alpha = \tau / I = 20.0 / 4.0 = 5.0 \, \text{rad/s}^2 \). Option A inverts the ratio (I/τ), option D multiplies τ and I (confusing with τ = Iα rearranged incorrectly).

explanations_tiered.definition: Newton's second law for rotation: torque equals moment of inertia times angular acceleration.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Rearrange τ = Iα to find angular acceleration α = τ/I.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A bigger twist (torque) makes the wheel spin up faster; a heavier wheel (larger I) resists spinning up.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Derived from vector form τ = Iα, where I is the moment of inertia about the axis of rotation.
reasoning_steps: Solve for α: α = τ / I.
Divide torque by moment of inertia.
misconceptions: Using α = I/τ (inverting ratio).
Multiplying τ × I instead of dividing.

flashcard.front: τ=20 N·m, I=4 kg·m². Find α.

concept_map_nodes.target: Larger τ → larger α, larger I → smaller α

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: τ=20 N·m, I=4 kg·m². Find α.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Analogous to a = F/m in linear motion.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Express torque in terms of moment of inertia and angular acceleration

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Kepler's second law (law of equal areas) is a direct consequence of which conservation principle?
questions.hint: The quantity \( L = m r v_\perp \) remains constant for a planet orbiting the Sun.
questions.explanation: Kepler's second law (equal areas in equal times) follows from conservation of angular momentum: no external torque about the Sun means angular momentum \( L = m r v_\perp \) is constant, which implies equal area swept per time. Option A (energy) is conserved but does not directly imply equal areas.

explanations_tiered.definition: Kepler's second law arises from conservation of angular momentum because the gravitational force is central (toward the Sun).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: No external torque about the Sun means angular momentum \( L = m r v_\perp \) is constant, which causes equal areas to be swept in equal times.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A planet swings faster when close to the Sun because it has to keep its "spin amount" (angular momentum) constant — like a spinning ice skater pulling in arms to spin faster.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Area swept per time = (1/2) r v_\perp. Since L = m r v_\perp constant, r v_\perp constant, so area rate is constant.
explanations_tiered.advanced: From \( dA/dt = (1/2) r^2 d\theta/dt = (1/2) r^2 \omega = L/(2m) \). With L constant, dA/dt constant.
reasoning_steps: Recall Kepler's second law: equal areas in equal times.
Recognize that central gravitational force exerts zero torque about the Sun.
Constant angular momentum directly implies constant area sweep rate.

formula_analysis.name: Area sweep rate
formula_analysis.derivation: From dA = ½ r² dθ and L = mr² ω.

misconceptions: Thinking energy conservation explains equal areas (energy is conserved but gives different relationship).
Confusing angular momentum with linear momentum.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Kepler II → conservation of angular momentum.

glossary_entry.definition: Consequence of conservation of angular momentum for a central force.

flashcard.front: Kepler's second law (equal areas) follows from which conservation law?
flashcard.back: Conservation of angular momentum (no torque about the Sun).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Kepler's second law is a consequence of which conservation law?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Conservation of angular momentum (central gravitational force gives zero torque).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Derive dA/dt = L/(2m) to show connection.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe the motion of the planets around the Sun
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Equal areas → constant angular momentum.
